input.prompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.content:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?
data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-14T01:08:29.161Z</current_datetime>

We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

input.initialPrompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.reasoningText:
The user has migrated from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform and is getting exit code 8 when test assemblies produce zero tests. This is a known behavioral difference - Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns a non-zero exit code (8) when no tests are discovered, whereas VSTest treated this as a success.

Let me look at their test project to understand the setup and provide the fix.

data.content:
Exit code 8 means "no tests found." Microsoft.Testing.Platform treats zero discovered tests as an error by default, unlike VSTest.

**Fix:** Add this property to your `.csproj`:

arguments.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>

<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>

toolCalls.name: edit
toolCalls.args:
{"path": "/tmp/sv-4e0f7312de694d0d98670193b2e4375d/TestProject.csproj", "old_str": "    <EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>", "new_str": "    <EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>"}

    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>

data.content: The property didn't take effect. Let me check the correct approach for this runner version:
data.reasoningText:
Hmm, it seems the property `TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests` might not be working as expected with this version, or it might not be the right property name. Let me check what the correct property or command-line option is.

Actually, looking more carefully at the output - the test runner still reports "Failed!" with 0 tests. The property I used might not be correct for this version of MSTest/Microsoft.Testing.Platform.

Let me research this. The correct approaches for Microsoft.Testing.Platform when no tests are found:

1. For MSTest runner (Microsoft.Testing.Platform), the command-line option is `--ignore-exit-code` with value 8 (the "no tests ran" exit code).


2. The MSBuild property might be different - I could try setting `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` to pass `--ignore-exit-code 8`, or there might be a newer property altogether. Looking at the recent documentation, this seems to be the right direction, though I need to verify the exact syntax. to 0 to allow tests to pass even when no tests are found. Let me explore what command-line options are actually available for the test runner.

data.content: Found it. There are two options: `--ignore-exit-code 8` or `--minimum-expected-tests 0`. The cleanest fix is setting `--minimum-expected-tests 0` via MSBuild. Let me update the project:

arguments.old_str:
<!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>
arguments.new_str:
<!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--minimum-expected-tests 0</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

<!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->

data.content: The argument was passed twice (from MSBuild property + CLI). The issue is clear. Let me use `--ignore-exit-code 8` instead, which is the correct approach for this scenario:

arguments.new_str:
<!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
arguments.old_str:
<!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--minimum-expected-tests 0</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

data.reasoningText:
The error is: `Option '--minimum-expected-tests' from provider 'Platform command line provider' (UID: PlatformCommandLineProvider) expects at most 1 arguments`

This is because `--minimum-expected-tests 0` is being passed TWICE (once from the MSBuild property `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` and once from the `--` command line argument). Let me fix this by removing the MSBuild property and using `--ignore-exit-code 8` instead, which seems more appropriate for this scenario.

Actually wait - looking more carefully at the log:
```
--minimum-expected-tests 0     --minimum-expected-tests 0


```

It's being passed twice because I have it both in the MSBuild property AND the command line. The error message says "expects at most 1 arguments" - this might mean it's a flag that takes 0 arguments (a boolean flag), or it could be that having it twice is the problem. Let me try with just `--ignore-exit-code 8` instead.

```

arguments.new_str:
<!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
arguments.old_str:
<!-- Treat zero discovered tests as success (matches VSTest behavior) -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--minimum-expected-tests 0</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

toolArgs.old_str:

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
1. <Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

data.content:
**Fixed.** The issue is that Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns exit code 8 when zero tests are discovered (VSTest returned 0). 

Add this to your `.csproj`:

```xml
<TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
```

This tells the runner to treat "no tests found" (exit code 8) as success, matching the old VSTest behavior. Actual test failures still produce a non-zero exit code.
